basmala: In the Name of God, the Infinitely Merciful, the Most Merciful.
intro: Al-Fīl — “The Elephant” — is an early Meccan sūra forming a close diptych with the next one, Quraysh. It recalls an event well known to Arab memory: the year 570, the very year in which, according to tradition, the Prophet was born. Abraha, the Abyssinian general ruling Yemen, had built a church in Sanʿāʾ to divert pilgrims from the Kaʿba; when his plan failed he marched on Mecca with the greatest army ever assembled in Arabia, with an elephant — a beast unknown to the Arabs — meant to bring down the Sanctuary. It is said that as the army approached Mecca the elephant knelt and would not be moved. And God sent against the army a swarm of birds bearing stones of baked clay. The army was destroyed. Only five verses, but they say one thing: the protection of the Kaʿba did not rest on human strength, but on God's design — already preparing the coming of His final Messenger.

versets.ar: أَلَمْ تَرَ كَيْفَ فَعَلَ رَبُّكَ بِأَصْحَابِ الْفِيلِ
versets.tr: a-lam tara kayfa faʿala rabbuka bi-aṣḥābi l-fīl
versets.trad: Have you not seen how your Lord dealt with the masters of the elephant?
versets.commentaire: aṣḥāb al-fīl (أَصْحَاب الفِيل): the masters of the elephant — Abraha's army. The story was familiar enough to the Arabs that the sūra does not tell it: it points to it as a public fact and asks the Prophet's contemporaries to draw the lesson. The text opens with a rhetorical question — a-lam tara (أَلَمْ تَرَ), “have you not seen” — that supposes a moral witness: in face of such a sign, how can one fail to acknowledge the hand of God?

mots.def: prefixed interrogative particle.
negative particle: negates the apocopated imperfect, the equivalent of a past negative.
raʾā: to see; in the apocopated imperfect after lam: “you have not seen”.
interrogative adverb: how?
faʿala: to do, to act, to accomplish.
rabb + suffix pronoun: your Lord.
ṣāḥib (sing.): companion; aṣḥāb (plur.): the people of, the masters of. Preposition bi- + noun in the genitive.
fīl: the elephant; word of Persian origin introduced into Arabic for this animal then unknown to Arabia.

versets.trad: Did He not make their scheming go astray,
versets.commentaire: kayd (كَيْد): cunning, scheming, plot — often applied in the Quran to the schemes of God's enemies. taḍlīl (تَضْلِيل): straying, leading into error. The verse turns the usual phrase: the very plotters are led astray by their own plot. It is one of the recurring laws of the Quran: God lets man carry his design through to the end — and that design turns back upon him.

mots.def: interrogative particle.
negative particle.
jaʿala: to make, to render, to place; in the apocopated imperfect after lam: “He has not made…”.
mots.gram: apocopated imperfect verb, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

mots.def: kayd: cunning, scheme, plot; with the plural suffix pronoun: their scheme.
preposition: in.
taḍlīl: verbal noun from ḍ-l-l; the leading astray, the falling into error.

versets.trad: and send against them birds in swarms,
versets.commentaire: ṭayran abābīl (طَيْرًا أَبَابِيلَ): birds in swarms, in successive bands. The word abābīl does not appear elsewhere in the Quran; commentators give it several senses — “in successive bands”, “in troops”, “coming from every direction”. Tradition describes sea-birds, swallows or herons, each carrying three small stones — one in each claw and one in the beak — that struck a man and undid him. The sūra does not linger on the marvel: it says simply that a great army was undone by what looked insignificant.

mots.def: conjunction: and.
arsala: to send, to dispatch, to release.
preposition ʿalā + plural suffix pronoun: upon them, against them.
ṭayr: bird (collective noun).
abābīl: rare word (hapax) of the Quran; in groups, in successive bands, in swarms.

versets.trad: pelting them with stones of baked clay.
versets.commentaire: sijjīl (سِجِّيل): a rare term, taken by some commentators to be a composition of two Persian words, sang (stone) and gil (clay) — hence the common meaning: stones of baked clay, hard as rock. Tradition adds that each stone was said to bear the name of the one it had to strike — the divine decree is precise, individual. Small in size, immense in effect: it takes little, once heaven has decided, to undo the calculations of the powerful.

mots.def: ramā: to throw, to hurl; in the imperfect feminine (ṭayr, “birds”, is taken as a feminine collective) + plural suffix pronoun.
mots.gram: imperfect verb + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. fem. sing.

mots.def: ḥajar: stone; ḥijāra: plural or collective (stones). Preposition bi- + noun in the genitive.
preposition: from, of; indicates the material.
sijjīl: stone of baked clay, hard; some commentators see in it a Persian composition (sang-gil, “stone-clay”).

versets.trad: He made them like devoured husks.
versets.commentaire: ʿaṣf maʾkūl (عَصْف مَّأْكُول): devoured husks — the remnants of leaves or straw that beasts have grazed, no more than scattered dust. The image is dense: the greatest army Arabia of that time had ever seen, reduced to what is most insignificant. The sūra closes on this brief comparison — and opens, by contrast, the next sūra, where the grace given to the Quraysh is not owed to their strength but to the Lord of this House.

mots.def: linking particle: so, thus.
jaʿala: to render, to make, to transform; perfect + plural suffix pronoun.
mots.gram: perfect verb + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

mots.def: preposition of comparison: like, as.
ʿaṣf: the green blades of cereals or their husks; chaff, the dry remains of harvests.
maʾkūl: passive participle of akala (to eat); that which has been eaten, devoured.
